1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean
4	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/StalkTensor.lean
5	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/Vestigial.lean
6	
7	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
8	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
9	
10	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
11	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
12	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
13	\[
14	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
15	  \qquad
16	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
17	\]
18	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
19	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
20	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
21	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
22	
23	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
24	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
25	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
26	% groups)
27	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
28	% SOURCE QUOTE:
29	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
30	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
31	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
32	% abelian groups defined by the formula
33	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
34	% \end{dfn}
35	%  [\dots]
36	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
37	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
38	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
39	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
40	% topologies  by
41	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
42	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
43	%  \end{dfn}"
44	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
45	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
46	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
47	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
48	homomorphisms.}
49	
50	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
51	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
52	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
53	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
54	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
55	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
56	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
57	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
58	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
59	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
60	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
61	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
62	structure. This is the content of
63	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
64	
65	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
66	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
67	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
68	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
69	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
70	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
71	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
72	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
73	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
74	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
75	\begin{enumerate}
76	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
77	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
78	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
79	    isomorphism classes;
80	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
81	    for \(\otimes\);
82	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
83	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
84	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
85	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
86	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
87	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
88	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
89	\end{enumerate}
90	At Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a
91	\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and
92	reading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route
93	conceivable. First, the category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves
94	of modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric)
95	monoidal category in Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)),
96	with the sectionwise relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)}
97	N(U)\); this applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
98	\(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
99	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
100	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
101	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
102	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\); sheafification
103	of presheaves of modules is the localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the
104	locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\), so a full monoidal structure on
105	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) would follow once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) were
106	supplied.
107	
108	This full-monoidal-category route is, however, \emph{not} the goal, and is not
109	pursued. The goal is the commutative \emph{group} on locally-trivial iso-classes
110	(à la \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so
111	it never needs a coherent monoidal category --- neither the
112	pentagon/triangle/hexagon identities nor the \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
113	that the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) API requires. What it needs is exactly the
114	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms of (3), and these are produced
115	directly: the associator by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
116	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
117	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), the unitors by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\)
118	pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise
119	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law
120	assembles \emph{by hand} the commutative group of \(\otimes\)-invertible
121	\(\otimes\)-iso-classes --- the Picard group
122	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) --- each group-axiom field fed a single
123	existence-of-isomorphism. This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
124	fixed-ring \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\),
125	whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent
126	monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) that
127	Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring; the present construction cannot take that
128	\(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route, since the coherent monoidal category on
129	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is exactly what it
130	avoids. The
131	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
132	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse existentially.
133	The \emph{full} \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) / \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) coherent
134	monoidal category is therefore not built for the group law and is off the critical
135	path; what the group law does still need from this circle of ideas is the
136	\emph{existence} of the associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), realized as a
137	sheafification transport whose single open left-whisker obligation is closed by the
138	stalk--tensor commutation (d.2). The stalk apparatus (d.1/d.2) is accordingly
139	\emph{on} the critical path, while the coherent monoidal category itself is not.
140	
141	\section{Mathlib API survey}
142	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
143	
144	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
145	(\texttt{b80f227}) were originally assembled into a single \emph{realization route}
146	(route~(e)): obtaining a full monoidal structure on the substrate by instantiating
147	Mathlib's abstract localization-of-monoidal-categories API rather than
148	hand-assembling the associator. That route is \emph{superseded}. The group law on
149	iso-classes never consumes a coherent monoidal category, and the associator is
150	obtained by direct gluing through \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, so the
151	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance on which route~(e) hinges is never required.
152	The survey below is retained as a Mathlib-API reference and to record why the route
153	is vestigial; the four Mathlib facts are all verified against the on-disk Mathlib
154	source.
155	
156	\paragraph{(i) The presheaf-of-modules monoidal category.} Mathlib's
157	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal} constructs a
158	monoidal-category instance
159	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\) on
160	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,(R \circ \mathtt{forget}_2\,\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,
161	\mathtt{RingCat})\), with the sectionwise relative tensor
162	\[
163	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
164	\]
165	% SOURCE: [Mathlib], `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct` /
166	% `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`, in
167	% `Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean` (L104, L125)
168	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean).
169	% SOURCE QUOTE:
170	% "noncomputable instance monoidalCategoryStruct :
171	%     MonoidalCategoryStruct (PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)) where
172	%   tensorObj := tensorObj
173	%   whiskerLeft _ _ _ g := tensorHom (𝟙 _) g
174	%   whiskerRight f _ := tensorHom f (𝟙 _)
175	%   ...
176	%   tensorUnit := unit _
177	%   associator M₁ M₂ M₃ := isoMk (fun _ ↦ α_ _ _ _) ...
178	% noncomputable instance monoidalCategory :
179	%     MonoidalCategory (PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)) where ..."
180	\textit{Source: [Mathlib], \texttt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct} /
181	\texttt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}, in
182	\texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean}, L104--L125.}
183	together with associator, left/right unitors and braiding making it symmetric
184	monoidal. The base presheaf of rings \(R \circ \mathtt{forget}_2\,
185	\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,\mathtt{RingCat}\) is exactly the project's
186	\(\mathtt{Sheaf.val}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) carrier (equal by \(\mathtt{rfl}\)),
187	so this monoidal structure applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
188	\(\mathcal{O}_X\) --- no project work. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
189	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
190	
191	\paragraph{(ii) The localization-of-monoidal-categories API.} Mathlib's
192	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.Basic} provides the class
193	\(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) and the construction
194	\(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\). The class records exactly
195	the whiskering-stability data:
196	% SOURCE: [Mathlib], `CategoryTheory.MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal` and
197	% `CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal`, in
198	% `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Localization/Monoidal/Basic.lean` (L44, L86, L440)
199	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Localization/Monoidal/Basic.lean).
200	% SOURCE QUOTE:
201	% "class IsMonoidal : Prop extends W.IsMultiplicative where
202	%   whiskerLeft (X : C) {Y₁ Y₂ : C} (g : Y₁ ⟶ Y₂) (hg : W g) : W (X ◁ g)
203	%   whiskerRight {X₁ X₂ : C} (f : X₁ ⟶ X₂) (hf : W f) (Y : C) : W (f ▷ Y)
204	%  ...
205	% def LocalizedMonoidal (L : C ⥤ D) (W : MorphismProperty C)
206	%     [W.IsMonoidal] [L.IsLocalization W] {unit : D} (_ : L.obj (𝟙_ C) ≅ unit) := D
207	%  ...
208	% noncomputable instance :
209	%     MonoidalCategory (LocalizedMonoidal L W ε) where ..."
210	\textit{Source: [Mathlib], \texttt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal} and
211	\texttt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}, in
212	\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Localization/Monoidal/Basic.lean}, L44--L440.}
213	Given \([\MonoidalCategory\,C]\), \([W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}]\), a localization
214	functor \(L : C \to D\) with \([L.\mathtt{IsLocalization}\,W]\) and an isomorphism
215	\(\varepsilon : L(\mathbf{1}_C) \cong \mathtt{unit}\), the localized category
216	\(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\,L\,W\,\varepsilon\) is a \(\MonoidalCategory\) with
217	\emph{all} coherence (associator, unitors, pentagon, triangle, naturalities)
218	derived. There is \emph{no} \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) anywhere in the
219	hypothesis chain.
220	
221	\paragraph{(iii) The proof template for $W$-monoidality.} Mathlib's
222	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW} proves
223	\((J.W\,(A := A)).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) \emph{stalkwise} for presheaves valued in a
224	\emph{fixed} monoidal concrete category \(A\) (i.e.\ \(C^{op} \to A\)), via the
225	conservative-family characterisation \(\mathtt{hP.W\_iff}\),
226	\(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.map\_tensor}\) and typeclass inference:
227	% SOURCE: [Mathlib], `CategoryTheory.ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal_W`
228	% and the `(J.W).IsMonoidal` instance, in
229	% `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean` (L48, L57)
230	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean).
231	% SOURCE QUOTE:
232	% "lemma ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal_W
233	%     [J.HasSheafCompose (forget A)] :
234	%     (J.W (A := A)).IsMonoidal :=
235	%   .mk' _ (fun f g hf hg ↦ by
236	%     simp only [hP.W_iff (A := A)] at hf hg ⊢
237	%     intro Φ
238	%     rw [Functor.Monoidal.map_tensor]
239	%     infer_instance)"
240	\textit{Source: [Mathlib],
241	\texttt{ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal\_W}, in
242	\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean}, L48--L57.}
243	This is the \emph{template} for the proof technique used in route~(e), but it does
244	\emph{not} apply directly to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\): modules over a
245	\emph{varying} ring are not of the form \(C^{op} \to A\) for a fixed monoidal
246	\(A\).
247	
248	\paragraph{The gap and route~(e), superseded.} The substrate
249	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\) of this chapter is the
250	sheafification of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\), and
251	sheafification of presheaves of modules is exactly the localization
252	\(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\). Route~(e)
253	proposed to obtain a \emph{full} monoidal structure on
254	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)
255	by applying \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) (ii) to the already-monoidal category (i),
256	which would require the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (its two
257	whiskering-stability fields \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}, with load-bearing
258	local-injectivity half \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}). This is
259	\emph{not} how the group law is realized. The group law consumes only the
260	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms, and the associator is built
261	directly by gluing canonical local isomorphisms through
262	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}); it never invokes
263	the \emph{full} \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance or a coherent monoidal category.
264	The \emph{full monoidal-category} route~(e) is therefore off the critical path. The
265	associator does, however, consume a \emph{single} left-whisker existence lemma
266	\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, closed by the d.2 stalk--tensor
267	commutation; so the stalk apparatus (d.1/d.2) itself is on the critical path, even
268	though the full coherent monoidal category built from it is not.
269	
270	The stalk-infrastructure analysis that route~(e) turned on is recorded here for
271	reference only, since that route is superseded. The \emph{stalk module structure}
272	is already present in Mathlib: the file
273	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk} supplies, for a topological
274	space \(X\), a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) on \(X\) and a point
275	\(x \in X\), the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure on the stalk
276	\(\mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) of a presheaf of modules \(M\),
277	together with the germ--scalar compatibility \(\mathtt{germ\_smul}\); a linearity
278	packaging of the induced stalk map of a morphism was built project-side
279	(\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, the \emph{linearity half} of ingredient (d.1)).
280	Route~(e) additionally required a (d.1)-\emph{bridge} between the site-level
281	\(J.W\) and the topological stalkwise-isomorphism criterion on
282	\(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\), and (d.2) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
283	\otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk with the relative module-presheaf tensor; there is
284	moreover no monoidal \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) in Mathlib. The full \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) coherent monoidal category that route~(e) would
285	build is not pursued; but the (d.1)-linearity packaging and the (d.2) stalk--tensor
286	commutation are \emph{on} the critical path, since the associator's single open
287	left-whisker obligation \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk} is closed
288	precisely by (d.2) (\cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}). What is superseded is only the
289	\emph{pair}-of-whiskering-fields \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) packaging and the
290	\(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category, not the stalk infrastructure.
291	
292	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
293	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
294	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
295	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
296	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
297	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
298	independently of any upstream timing.
299	
300	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
301	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
302	
303	\begin{definition}
304	
305	
306	\leanok
307	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
308	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
309	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
310	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
311	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
312	  \[
313	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
314	  \]
315	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
316	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
317	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
318	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
319	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
320	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
321	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
322	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
323	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
324	  canonical compatibility
325	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
326	     \;\cong\;
327	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
328	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
329	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
330	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
331	
332	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
333	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
334	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
335	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
336	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
337	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
338	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
339	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
340	\end{definition}
341	
342	\begin{lemma}
343	
344	
345	\leanok
346	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
347	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
348	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
349	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
350	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
351	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
352	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
353	  \[
354	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
355	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
356	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
357	  \]
358	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
359	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
360	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
361	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
362	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
363	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
364	  \[
365	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
366	    \qquad
367	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
368	  \]
369	  the associator
370	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
371	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
372	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
373	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
374	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
375	\end{lemma}
376	
377	\begin{proof}
378	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
379	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
380	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
381	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
382	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
383	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
384	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
385	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
386	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
387	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
388	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
389	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
390	\end{proof}
391	
392	\begin{remark}
393	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
394	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
395	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
396	        lem:whisker_of_W}
397	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
398	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
399	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
400	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
401	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
402	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
403	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
404	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
405	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
406	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
407	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
408	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
409	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
410	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
411	
412	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
413	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
414	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
415	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
416	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
417	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
418	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
419	  fixed-base presheaves in
420	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
421	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
422	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
423	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). The \emph{full}
424	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance and the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal
425	  category are not on the critical path: the group law is realized without them. The
426	  single left-whisker existence lemma and its underlying d.1/d.2 stalk apparatus, by
427	  contrast, \emph{are} on the critical path, since the associator's sheafification
428	  transport consumes exactly that one whisker obligation
429	  (\cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, closed via d.2).
430	
431	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
432	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
433	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
434	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
435	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
436	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
437	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
438	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
439	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
440	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
441	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
442	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
443	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
444	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The carrier --- iso-classes
445	  of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
446	  Mathlib's Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
447	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), but it
448	  is built \emph{differently}: Mathlib reads its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) off the
449	  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent monoidal category
450	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring,
451	  whereas here --- with no coherent monoidal category on
452	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) available for the varying structure sheaf --- the
453	  group is assembled by hand from the existence-of-isomorphism facts.
454	\end{remark}
455	
456	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
457	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
458	
459	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
460	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
461	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
462	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
463	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
464	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
465	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
466	are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
467	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
468	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
469	\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
470	and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
471	read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
472	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
473	records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
474	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
475	the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
476	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
477	critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
478	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
479	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
480	to the off-path route~(e).
481	
482	\begin{lemma}
483	
484	\leanok
485	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
486	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
487	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
488	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
489	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
490	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
491	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
492	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
493	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
494	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
495	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
496	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
497	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
498	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
499	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
500	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
501	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
502	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
503	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
504	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
505	  \[
506	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
507	      \;\;:=\;\;
508	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
509	  \]
510	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
511	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
512	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
513	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
514	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
515	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
516	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
517	\end{lemma}
518	
519	\begin{proof}
520	
521	
522	\leanok
523	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
524	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
525	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
526	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
527	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
528	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
529	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
530	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
531	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
532	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
533	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
534	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
535	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
536	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
537	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
538	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
539	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
540	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
541	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
542	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
543	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
544	
545	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
546	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
547	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
548	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
549	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
550	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
551	\end{proof}
552	
553	\begin{lemma}
554	
555	
556	\leanok
557	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
558	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
559	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
560	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
561	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
562	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
563	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
564	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
565	  morphism
566	  \[
567	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
568	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
569	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
570	  \]
571	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
572	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
573	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
574	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
575	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
576	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
577	\end{lemma}
578	
579	\begin{proof}
580	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
581	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
582	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
583	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
584	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
585	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
586	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
587	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
588	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
589	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
590	  isomorphism}
591	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
592	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
593	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
594	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
595	  therefore extension of scalars along
596	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
597	
598	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
599	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
600	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
601	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
602	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
603	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
604	  the abstract pullback functor.
605	
606	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
607	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
608	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
609	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
610	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
611	   \;\cong\;
612	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
613	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
614	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
615	  comparison
616	  \[
617	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
618	      \;\longrightarrow\;
619	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
620	      \;\otimes\;
621	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
622	  \]
623	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
624	
625	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
626	  % \ref{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback} (the pair $(f^*, f_*)$ of
627	  % pullback/pushforward of $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism of ringed
628	  % spaces is an adjoint pair)
629	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L252-273).
630	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
631	  % "\begin{lemma}
632	  % \label{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}
633	  % Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$
634	  % be a morphism of ringed spaces.
635	  % \begin{enumerate}
636	  % \item The functor
637	  % $f_* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y)$
638	  % is left exact. In fact it commutes with all limits.
639	  % \item The functor
640	  % $f^* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$
641	  % is right exact. In fact it commutes with all colimits.
642	  %  [\dots]
643	  % \end{enumerate}
644	  % \end{lemma}
645	  %  [\dots]
646	  % Parts (1) and (2) hold because $(f^*, f_*)$ is an adjoint pair
647	  % of functors, see
648	  % Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-adjoint-pullback-pushforward-modules}"
649	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
650	  \texttt{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}: pullback and pushforward of
651	  $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism form an adjoint pair $(f^*, f_*)$, the
652	  abstract universal property on which the comparison below rests.}
653	
654	  \emph{Step 3 (the genuine residual: H1 alone).} Completing this comparison at the
655	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise handle on the abstract functor
656	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left
657	  adjoint of pushforward (the presheaf-of-modules avatar of the
658	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) adjunction of the cited Stacks lemma) and admits no
659	  sectionwise formula in Mathlib at the pinned commit. The closure passes through
660	  two presheaf-level ingredients, of which exactly one now remains open.
661	
662	  The first ingredient --- a strong-monoidal upgrade of
663	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the structure-sheaf ring \emph{isomorphism}
664	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\), so that base change along the isomorphism commutes with
665	  \(\otimes\) --- is \emph{closed in this chapter} as
666	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
667	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}\)): for a ring isomorphism
668	  \(e : R \simeq S\) the canonical map \(a \otimes_R b \mapsto a \otimes_S b\) is an
669	  \(R\)-linear equivalence \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R
670	  \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M
671	  \otimes_S N)\).
672	
673	  The \emph{sole remaining residual} is therefore the presheaf-level identification
674	  \[
675	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \;\cong\; \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
676	  \]
677	  via \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\). One exhibits a
678	  presheaf-level adjunction
679	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) and concludes
680	  that \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\), being a left adjoint of
681	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\), is canonically isomorphic to the abstract left
682	  adjoint \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\). Here \(\beta\) is the structure map of the
683	  restriction functor, so that \((M|_f).\mathrm{val} =
684	  (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathrm{obj}\,M.\mathrm{val}\) definitionally;
685	  composing the resulting comparison with
686	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv} yields the asserted isomorphism.
687	
688	  This presheaf-level adjunction is the presheaf analogue of the sheaf-level
689	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\), and must be built from
690	  presheaf-level \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and
691	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardCongr}\). These two building blocks are \emph{Mathlib-absent}
692	  at the pinned commit: only the \emph{sheaf}-level versions exist
693	  (\texttt{Mathlib/.../SheafOfModules/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean}), whereas
694	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardId}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) do already exist at the
695	  presheaf level. This residual is being constructed \emph{directly}, mirroring the
696	  existing sheaf-level template app-for-app at the presheaf level (a Mathlib-gradient
697	  build, not a deferral to an upstream PR).
698	
699	  This lemma is the single substrate linchpin of the relative Picard group law for
700	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(M, N\): the associator
701	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} consumes the comparison
702	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_f \cong M|_f \otimes_U N|_f\) on its sole consumer
703	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}, where the restriction is commuted
704	  past the \emph{sheafified} tensor \(M \otimes_X N\) \emph{before} any local
705	  trivialisation of \(M, N\) is introduced --- so the comparison is required for the
706	  global, untrivialised modules, and no free-cover specialisation can stand in for it.
707	  Consequently the H1 residual \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong
708	  \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) is genuinely on the critical path, and closing it is
709	  the \emph{single open substrate obligation} of this chapter. The superseded
710	  route~(e) \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal} \to \mathtt{Sheaf.monoidalCategory}\)
711	  (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}) remains named only as a last-resort fallback should the
712	  presheaf-level adjunction itself bottom out; it is not the plan.
713	\end{proof}
714	
715	\begin{lemma}
716	[Presheaf-level pushforward adjunction and strong-monoidal restriction (H1/H2 substrate)]
717	  \label{lem:presheaf_pushforward_adj_substrate}
718	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.pushforwardNatTrans,
719	        PresheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr,
720	        PresheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj,
721	        PresheafOfModules.isIso_of_isIso_app,
722	        PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsMonoidalOfBijective}
723	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
724	  These five presheaf-level declarations are the two ingredients on which the
725	  linchpin \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} rests; both are absent from Mathlib at
726	  the pinned commit and are supplied project-side.
727	
728	  \emph{H1 (the pushforward adjunction).} For a morphism \(\beta\) of presheaves of
729	  rings, \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardCongr}\) are the
730	  presheaf-level de-sheafifications of Mathlib's sheaf-level
731	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardNatTrans}\) and
732	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr}\)
733	  (\texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean}),
734	  reproduced app-for-app one level down at the presheaf of modules. From them
735	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\) assembles the presheaf-level adjunction
736	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\), the
737	  presheaf analogue of the sheaf-level
738	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\). Pairing this against the
739	  existing \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction}\) through
740	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) yields the canonical isomorphism
741	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) of two left

2563	
2564	\section{Pullback-monoidality: invertibility under a general scheme morphism}
2565	\label{sec:tensorobj_pullback_monoidality}
2566	
2567	The relative Picard consumer (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}) re-bases its
2568	line-bundle carrier onto the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
2569	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}, and its
2570	functorial structure maps are \emph{pullback} maps of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules: the
2571	projection pullback \(\pi_T^*\) along \(C \times_S T \to T\) and the base-change
2572	pullback along \(C \times_S T' \to C \times_S T\). For these maps to descend to the
2573	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) carrier one needs that \emph{pullback preserves
2574	\(\otimes\)-invertibility} for \emph{general} morphisms of schemes --- the
2575	projection and the base-change morphism are neither open immersions nor flat.
2576	
2577	This is a genuinely different fact from the open-immersion compatibility
2578	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, and not a corollary of it. That lemma handles
2579	restriction along an open immersion, and it does so by routing through the
2580	\emph{lax right} adjoint \(\mathtt{pushforward}/\mathtt{restrictScalars}\), which is
2581	strong monoidal there \emph{only} because an open immersion's structure-sheaf ring
2582	map is a local \emph{isomorphism} (\(f.\mathtt{appIso}\)). A general scheme
2583	morphism has no such isomorphism, so the right-adjoint route is unavailable. The
2584	general case instead uses the \emph{left} adjoint --- pullback \(=\) inverse image
2585	\(=\) extension of scalars --- which is strong monoidal for \emph{any} ring map,
2586	with no iso hypothesis: extension of scalars \(- \otimes_R S\) along \(R \to S\)
2587	carries the tensorator \((M \otimes_R N) \otimes_R S \cong (M \otimes_R S)
2588	\otimes_S (N \otimes_R S)\) unconditionally. This is the whole reason the general
2589	lemma is a separate result.
2590	
2591	\begin{lemma}
2592	[Pullback commutes with $\otimes$ for a general scheme morphism]
2593	  \label{lem:pullback_tensor_iso}
2594	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackTensorIso}
2595	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2596	  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let
2597	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
2598	  Writing \(f^* = \mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f\) for the inverse-image functor
2599	  of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules, the canonical comparison
2600	  \[
2601	    f^*(M \otimes_X N)
2602	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
2603	    (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N)
2604	  \]
2605	  is an isomorphism, natural in \(M\) and \(N\). No local-freeness, flatness, or
2606	  open-immersion hypothesis is required: it holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
2607	  and all morphisms \(f\).
2608	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
2609	  % \ref{lemma-tensor-product-pullback} (pullback of $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a
2610	  % morphism of ringed spaces commutes with tensor product, functorially)
2611	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L2392-2400).
2612	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2613	  % "\begin{lemma}
2614	  % \label{lemma-tensor-product-pullback}
2615	  % Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be
2616	  % a morphism of ringed spaces. Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$
2617	  % be $\mathcal{O}_Y$-modules. Then
2618	  % $f^*(\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_Y} \mathcal{G})
2619	  % = f^*\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} f^*\mathcal{G}$
2620	  % functorially in $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$.
2621	  % \end{lemma}"
2622	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
2623	  \texttt{lemma-tensor-product-pullback}: for a morphism of ringed spaces
2624	  \(f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)\) and \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules
2625	  \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\), one has \(f^*(\mathcal{F} \otimes \mathcal{G}) =
2626	  f^*\mathcal{F} \otimes f^*\mathcal{G}\) functorially.}
2627	\end{lemma}
2628	
2629	\begin{proof}
2630	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2631	  The proof mirrors the structure of \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} but routes
2632	  through the \emph{left} adjoint, where no isomorphism hypothesis on the ring map
2633	  is needed.
2634	
2635	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce to the presheaf level).} By
2636	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}, the substrate tensor \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
2637	  sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
2638	  \(M.\mathrm{val} \otimes^{p} N.\mathrm{val}\), where
2639	  \(\otimes^{p} = \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\). The pullback
2640	  commutes with sheafification: the genuine Mathlib lemma
2641	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\) --- which is general
2642	  (it holds for any morphism of ring-sheaves, and is already consumed in the
2643	  open-immersion case \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) --- supplies a natural
2644	  isomorphism moving \(f^*\) inside the sheafification. The claim therefore reduces
2645	  to the presheaf-level comparison
2646	  \[
2647	    (\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,(A \otimes^{p} B)
2648	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
2649	    \bigl((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A\bigr)
2650	      \;\otimes^{p}\;
2651	    \bigl((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B\bigr),
2652	  \]
2653	  where \(\varphi\) is the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of rings
2654	  induced by \(f\), and \(A = M.\mathrm{val}\), \(B = N.\mathrm{val}\).
2655	
2656	  \emph{Step 2 (the presheaf pullback is strong monoidal).} The presheaf-level
2657	  pullback \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\) is, sectionwise,
2658	  \emph{extension of scalars} along the section ring maps of \(\varphi\) --- the
2659	  left adjoint of \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\). Mathlib equips extension of scalars
2660	  along \emph{any} ring homomorphism \(\psi\) with a strong-monoidal structure,
2661	  \((\mathtt{extendScalars}\,\psi).\mathtt{Monoidal}\)
2662	  (\texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Monoidal/Adjunction.lean}); its
2663	  tensorator is the canonical isomorphism
2664	  \((M \otimes_R N) \otimes_R S \cong (M \otimes_R S) \otimes_S (N \otimes_R S)\),
2665	  valid with \emph{no} hypothesis on \(\psi\). Assembling these sectionwise
2666	  tensorators against the presheaf restriction morphisms furnishes a strong-monoidal
2667	  structure on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), whose monoidal
2668	  comparison \(\mu\) is precisely the presheaf-level isomorphism required by Step~1.
2669	  Sheafifying \(\mu\) and transporting along the
2670	  \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\) isomorphism of Step~1 yields the asserted
2671	  isomorphism \(f^*(M \otimes_X N) \cong (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N)\). Naturality in
2672	  \(M, N\) is inherited from the naturality of \(\mu\) and of sheafification.
2673	
2674	  The contrast with \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is exactly the adjoint used:
2675	  there the comparison is produced from the \emph{right} adjoint
2676	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}/\mathtt{restrictScalars}\), which is strong monoidal only
2677	  because the open immersion makes the comparison ring map a (local) isomorphism;
2678	  here the \emph{left} adjoint \(\mathtt{extendScalars}\) is strong monoidal for
2679	  every ring map, so the hypothesis of an isomorphism is dropped entirely.
2680	\end{proof}
2681	
2682	\begin{lemma}
2683	[Pullback preserves the structure sheaf]
2684	  \label{lem:pullback_unit_iso}
2685	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackUnitIso}
2686	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2687	  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes. The pullback of the structure sheaf
2688	  is the structure sheaf: there is a canonical isomorphism
2689	  \[
2690	    f^*\bigl(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)
2691	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
2692	    \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,Y.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf},
2693	    \qquad\text{i.e.}\qquad
2694	    f^* \mathcal{O}_X \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_Y,
2695	  \]
2696	  where \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf} = \mathcal{O}_X\) is
2697	  the monoidal unit of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\).
2698	\end{lemma}
2699	
2700	\begin{proof}
2701	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2702	  The monoidal unit is preserved by the strong-monoidal pullback of
2703	  \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso}. At the section level the relevant comparison is the
2704	  unit comparison \(\varepsilon\) of the strong-monoidal extension of scalars
2705	  \((\mathtt{extendScalars}\,\psi).\mathtt{Monoidal}\): for a ring map
2706	  \(\psi : R \to S\) it is the canonical isomorphism \(R \otimes_R S \cong S\)
2707	  exhibiting that extension of scalars carries the base ring \(R\) (the
2708	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) unit over \(R\)) to \(S\) (the unit over \(S\)). Assembling
2709	  these sectionwise unit comparisons across the presheaf and sheafifying descends to
2710	  the asserted isomorphism \(f^*\mathcal{O}_X \cong \mathcal{O}_Y\).
2711	
2712	  A morphism \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit} : f^*\mathcal{O}_X \to
2713	  \mathcal{O}_Y\) already exists in Mathlib (it is consumed in
2714	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial.pullback}\), where it is shown to be an isomorphism for
2715	  an open immersion by a finality argument on the comma category of the open
2716	  cover). For a \emph{general} morphism \(f\) that finality argument is unavailable,
2717	  so whether \(\mathtt{pullbackObjUnitToUnit}\) is itself an isomorphism in general
2718	  must be checked separately; if it is not directly available as an iso, the iso is
2719	  obtained instead from the \(\mathtt{extendScalars}\) unit comparison
2720	  \(\varepsilon\) above, which is an isomorphism for every ring map and hence
2721	  descends to one for every \(f\).
2722	\end{proof}
2723	
2724	\begin{lemma}
2725	[Pullback preserves $\otimes$-invertibility for a general scheme morphism]
2726	  \label{lem:isinvertible_pullback}
2727	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.IsInvertible.pullback}
2728	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible, lem:pullback_tensor_iso,
2729	        lem:pullback_unit_iso}
2730	  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let
2731	  \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). If \(M\) is \(\otimes\)-invertible
2732	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), then so is its pullback \(f^*M\):
2733	  \[
2734	    \mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)
2735	      \;\Longrightarrow\;
2736	    \mathtt{IsInvertible}\bigl((\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,f).\mathrm{obj}\,M\bigr).
2737	  \]
2738	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
2739	  % \ref{lemma-pullback-invertible} (the pullback of an invertible module is
2740	  % invertible) (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4142-4147).
2741	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2742	  % "\begin{lemma}
2743	  % \label{lemma-pullback-invertible}
2744	  % Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be a
2745	  % morphism of ringed spaces. The pullback $f^*\mathcal{L}$ of an
2746	  % invertible $\mathcal{O}_Y$-module is invertible.
2747	  % \end{lemma}"
2748	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
2749	  \texttt{lemma-pullback-invertible}: the pullback \(f^*\mathcal{L}\) of an
2750	  invertible \(\mathcal{O}\)-module along a morphism of ringed spaces is
2751	  invertible.}
2752	\end{lemma}
2753	
2754	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4149-4157).
2755	% "\begin{proof}
2756	% By Lemma \ref{lemma-invertible}
2757	% there exists an $\mathcal{O}_Y$-module $\mathcal{N}$ such that
2758	% $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_Y} \mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_Y$.
2759	% Pulling back we get
2760	% $f^*\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} f^*\mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_X$
2761	% by Lemma \ref{lemma-tensor-product-pullback}.
2762	% Thus $f^*\mathcal{L}$ is invertible by Lemma \ref{lemma-invertible}.
2763	% \end{proof}"
2764	\begin{proof}
2765	  \uses{lem:pullback_tensor_iso, lem:pullback_unit_iso}
2766	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Lemma \texttt{lemma-pullback-invertible}.}
2767	  This is the Stacks proof in the project's notation. By
2768	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) there is a
2769	  witness \(N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) together with an isomorphism
2770	  \(e : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\). Take the pullback witness
2771	  \((\mathtt{pullback}\,f).\mathrm{obj}\,N = f^*N\). The required isomorphism
2772	  exhibiting \(f^*M\) as \(\otimes\)-invertible is the composite
2773	  \[
2774	    (f^*M) \otimes_Y (f^*N)
2775	      \;\xrightarrow[\sim]{\;\mathtt{pullbackTensorIso}^{-1}\;}\;
2776	    f^*(M \otimes_X N)
2777	      \;\xrightarrow[\sim]{\;f^*e\;}\;
2778	    f^*\mathcal{O}_X
2779	      \;\xrightarrow[\sim]{\;\mathtt{pullbackUnitIso}\;}\;
2780	    \mathcal{O}_Y,
2781	  \]
2782	  This composite uses \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso} to commute the pullback past the
2783	  tensor and \cref{lem:pullback_unit_iso} to identify \(f^*\mathcal{O}_X\) with
2784	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y\). Hence \(f^*N\) is a tensor inverse of \(f^*M\), so
2785	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(f^*M)\) holds.
2786	\end{proof}

2788	\section{The invertibility-carrier Picard group}
2789	\label{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}
